region_of_origin: Middle East / Eastern Mediterranean (Lebanon, Syria, Turkey, Greece)
food_subgroup: Stuffed vegetable dishes — rice and herb filling wrapped in vine leaves

ontology.foodex2_description: Stuffed vine leaves (dolma) — rice-based filling wrapped in grape/vine leaves
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for stuffed vine leaves; A0FKP (stuffed vegetables) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for stuffed vine leaves as a prepared food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stuffed vine leaves consists of 6 individual rolls, weighing approximately 180 g in total (approximately 30 g per piece).

nutrient_source.title: Dolma (stuffed grape leaves, rice filling) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central does not carry a single exact entry for restaurant-style stuffed vine leaves with pine nuts. Values are derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Grape leaves, stuffed with rice' (FDC ID 173944) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods data for rice, pine nuts, olive oil, and herbs. The composite per-100 g values reflect a rice-dominant filling (approximately 60% cooked white rice, 10% pine nuts, 20% vine leaves, 10% herbs and olive oil). Sodium is elevated to reflect typical restaurant preparation with added salt and lemon. Values are consistent with published Middle Eastern food composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stuffed vine leaves as a composite dish. GI estimated at 35 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice cooked with olive oil and wrapped in vine leaves. Cooking rice with fat and acid (lemon juice) and serving chilled significantly lowers the GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72) due to retrogradation of starch and fat-mediated slowing of gastric emptying. The University of Sydney GI Database lists cold cooked rice at GI 53 and rice dishes with fat at GI 35–45. An estimate of 35 (low GI) is defensible for this preparation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 35.6) / 100 ≈ 12.5, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 19.8 × 1.8 = 35.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 310 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 558 mg sodium per 6-piece restaurant serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should be aware that a single restaurant serving of stuffed vine leaves contributes approximately 558 mg sodium (about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and portion control or requesting reduced-salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to chilled rice, olive oil, and lemon juice reducing starch digestibility
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stuffed vine leaves have a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a moderate glycemic load of 12.5 per serving, making them a relatively suitable carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil and pine nuts; low saturated fat; cholesterol-free; contains plant sterols and antioxidants from herbs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil and pine nut fat profile (predominantly monounsaturated) is consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stuffed vine leaves, known across the Middle East and Eastern Mediterranean as dolma, warak dawali, or warak enab, are a classic dish consisting of tender grape or vine leaves wrapped around a fragrant filling of cooked white rice, pine nuts, fresh herbs (typically parsley, mint, and dill), olive oil, lemon juice, onion, and warm spices such as allspice, cinnamon, and black pepper. The rolls are simmered until the leaves are soft and the rice is fully cooked, then served chilled or at room temperature. The texture is soft and yielding with a slightly chewy leaf exterior, and the flavour profile is herbaceous, lemony, and mildly nutty from the pine nuts. In the Singapore context, this dish is served at Middle Eastern and Lebanese restaurants such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant at Boat Quay, typically as a cold appetiser or mezze item in portions of six pieces. Nutritionally, stuffed vine leaves are a moderate-energy food at approximately 142 kcal per 100 g, with a low glycemic index estimated at 35 due to the chilling of the rice (starch retrogradation) and the presence of olive oil and lemon acid, which slow gastric emptying. The fat profile is predominantly monounsaturated, consistent with Mediterranean dietary patterns associated with cardiovascular benefit. Sodium is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and contains pine nuts as the primary allergen. It is a good source of dietary fibre, folate, iron, and vitamin E.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific restaurant preparation exists in a primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for stuffed grape leaves (FDC ID 173944) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods component data. GI is an analogous estimate based on cold cooked rice with fat and acid from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is a recognised approved source and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173944, grape leaves stuffed with rice) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods for pine nuts and olive oil components. The restaurant version at Sahara Bar & Restaurant includes pine nuts, which are reflected in the fat and energy values. 2. Sodium is estimated at 310 mg per 100 g to reflect typical restaurant-level salting; the USDA SR Legacy entry reports approximately 290–320 mg per 100 g for comparable preparations. 3. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 (low) based on cold cooked rice with olive oil and lemon, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database data for chilled rice dishes with fat. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products; naturally zero. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a plant-based dish with no fortified ingredients; naturally zero. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; olive oil and pine nuts do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. Serving size of 180 g (6 pieces at approximately 30 g each) is based on standard restaurant portion sizing for Middle Eastern mezze. 9. The dish is served chilled per the input description, which is consistent with the low GI estimate. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for the identified ingredients and are not directly measured for this specific restaurant preparation.
